item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is one of my new favourite scips. It's simple, builds the tension while still getting the point across in a good amount of time, and does a good job of playing the red herring.

Enthusiastic +1 from me.

item.title: [REDACTED BY AARS538]
item.content:encoded:
Not related to the piece (which gets a +1 from me btw). But check this out:

AARS538
A-ARS5-38
A-ARSE-38

Maybe this explains why AARS538 has been going off the rails. It's pissed because it has a silly name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The primary point of this SCP isn't actually the memetic diseases themselves. I would suggest you read to the end of the article before passing judgement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, but it's gonna be a hard downvote for me. I was reading through the list of memetic diseases to the point where I realized it was the same thing as a 1st grade math formula machine - just plugging in weird or nonsensical stuff, and having a death count as an output.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I've just never read 370 before, but that's an interesting concept. Perhaps AARS538 doesn't have full access to other cognitohazard files?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is SCP-3281-T-e related to SCP-370-a? Because while it's not an exact match, it brought it to mind, and the thought of the program failing to recognize the connection is compelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the possible concepts behind AARS (not necessarily the canon one) is that it's not malfunctioning at all. The way it's acting is actually the ideal way for it to manage SCP-3281, down to hiding some actions from the Foundation.

Building a backup system is the primary plan, based on the report, but remember that if AARS found out someone was making another system to record information about SCP-3281, it probably would respond pretty aggressively.

As for T-b, it has a transmissibility rating of A+. You can imagine that the particular meme spreads quickly enough that A) it would need to be dealt with as quickly as possible (so lethally) and B) it rapidly affects large amounts of people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was an interesting read.
This mental 'disease' is interesting on the face of it, and the use of a computer to analyse and manage containment is quite logical. I can also see why human oversight of AARS538 would be a bad idea. The overseers would catch 3281.
Have a +1 for a well-constructed and thought-out article.

A question: What is SCP-3281-T-b that it has to be completely contained? And why did AARS think it called for nearly 12,000 killings? That seems very extreme. My speculation is that it's either really bad, or AARS is glitching out big time over this one.

+ some analysis of AARS538
– hide block

The report implies that AARS has come off the rails, but leaves it ambiguous as to the degree and cause of that fault.
As to the cause, internal corruption doesn't seem all that likely to me. I'm no expert on any of this, but I (like to think I) know a little about reliability of computers, and they don't normally go wrong on their own, unless they've been spectacularly badly programmed. I do know that data in memory(RAM) can be corrupted by cosmic radiation, but that doesn't happen very often, as the rays usually don't get through the Earth's magnetoshpere and atmosphere, and they can easily go straight through an IC and not do anything. It's vanishingly rare, especially these days. And I think there are actually technologies that can minimise what little damage a cosmic ray impact would do. Error-correcting memory for RAM, and RAID arrays for hard disk storage are some examples.

But if this is critical, why didn't the Foundation use a slightly brute-force strategy, and build a second, identical system, operating in parallel? That way, if one goes crackers, the other one could flag it down. Maybe there'd need to be three for this sort of thing to work, and that might be prohibitive, so maybe a smaller system, designed explicitly to supervise AARS, and flag it down if it goes crackers. Maybe that would be appropriate.
But that would circumvent the creep factor.

I suppose what I'm trying to say is that I find it hard to believe that AARS went crazy on its own. I think it must have been either outside tampering that caused this behaviour or AARS is a slightly unstable AI(Like Glacon in the AIAD canon). If it is an AI, its been cooped up by itself for so long, it might have gone stir-crazy or something like that.

A thought just occurred to me. What if this is only the start of AARS's long slide into insanity? That's terrifying.


Some people have assumed AARS538 to be an AI, but it isn't actually stated that it is one. I can see why you'd assume that, but still, the possibility is there that it's a dumber computerized system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not feeling it. I've never had a particular problem with compulsion effects, but the anomaly is nothing but a bog standard compulsion meme, and the "Foundation Cognitohazard A.I. goes rogue" aspect falls flat on its face for me.

Maybe making the memetic actions more absurd or insidious could at least make the article more interesting, but otherwise, the concept isn't wooing me.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of them involves a piggy bank, but I won't say which.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, this one isn't doing it for me. Too many redactions in the logs, and it ends with "oh noes the foundation itself is affected". Nothing here gets me interested in the article, nothing sticks with me after I'm finished, and it just felt like a chore to read through.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, I did fill in the blanks for two of them in the supplementary report. If I had made it so any of the others were obvious, it would kind of defeat the purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The logs are too long to be fully effective - in combination with the redaction, they become pretty repetitive. The redaction itself is over-used to the extent that it becomes unintentionally comic - it's like you're forcing me to play madlibs, rather than directing me to disturbing possibilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The storytelling here is massively good, to the point where I could forgive being irritated by the pacing a bit (seemed to go on a bit too long IMO).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really good use of elision, but the logs (in particular the -A section) could either be pruned or spiced up with some odd or insidious details. At the moment they seem repetitive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty torn on this one.

+ show block
– hide block

Cons:
It's ultimately a combination of the massive redaction trope with a compulsion effect. That's not a fair full assessment, because there's still a ton of very good storytelling going on around those, but the persistent brain damage just keeps dragging me back into "bad compulsion effect" territory, and it takes me out of it. It might help if you had a more specific technical definition of the brain damage. That is, is it similar to spongiform encephalopathies (aka holes appearing in the brain) or hypoxic brain damage (cells in isolated brain areas drying from lack of oxygen)? Also, if it's affecting cognition and impulse control, the damage would probably be in the pre-frontal cortex.

I'm not saying your description is incorrect or unscientific (It's actually quite good… for a compulsion effect) I just think more detail would make it feel less like a generic boil-your-brain memetic compulsion effect, even if it's functionally still the same thing.

The other con would just be that 11k casualties is a really big number. I don't… quite buy it. Yes, I know, SCP-579-level spooky dread, but you've already spent plenty of wordcount loading up the casualties, so popping down a bigger number doesn't have as much impact.

On the pro side, as I said, the surrounding storytelling is still excellent. I guess I might've been looking for another reveal or story direction at the end of the investigation document, but it's still very neat. I also enjoy imagining drones with rubber bullets flying around frantically searching for people near phone booths.

So, no vote.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: The image used is my own creation, with the use of this website to distort the text at the center. The rest is basically just excessive use of GIMP.

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page. If the images are not wholly your own, please post the image's source as a link (or links) in reply to this post and edit it into your first post in this discussion thread.

Edit: Problem resolved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a fair justification for it, although I do agree that it's probably not sophisticated enough to keep out the system. It does justify the fact that it creates separate dialogues, which was my main concern about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had (sometimes still have) a similar anxiety about arbitrary thoughts or actions, which is how I first got the idea for this SCP.
The idea was that the captcha was meant to keep out AARS538, because of how it could respond if it found out Foundation agents were investigating it and finding out details about SCP-3281. Realistically, it'd probably take more to prevent such a (presumably) sophisticated system out, but I had to make it so people could actually get to the document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a child, I had an irrational fear that if I said something nobody had said before in a place nobody had ever said it, I would accidentally summon a demon but there'd be no way to know it was going to happen because I was the first person to ever try it. The anomaly on its face sort of resembles this concept; arbitrary actions, unknown to the public, have supernatural consequences.

This treatment of that concept is compelling enough for an upvote, but it's even worse to see that even the Foundation doesn't have a full understanding of what's happening or how it works. I don't know how I feel about the report generation gimmick (who is captcha keeping out? the filtering AI itself?) but the report added another dimension to an article I already liked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an idea I've been working on for a few months, now. It's my second (successful) SCP and my first SCP in several years, so I'm excited to see how it will go over. Hopefully the concepts I meant to communicate are clear enough, but if there are parts that don't make sense, let me know.

Because I've been incubating this particular draft for so long, I don't actually remember the usernames of everyone who's helped critique it, with the exception of Communism will win, but if you've given me feedback, thank you very much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, maybe I shouldn't have jumped on this as soon as it came up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you may have caught me in the middle of uploading the file to the page. Hopefully everything is showing up properly now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really could have gone in the 3000 contest, but it's just as effective here. A computer program killing people without accountability or oversight, messing important things up, and being allowed to do it because there's no better option yet? Chilling and topical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The .jpg is broken, Also it needs a caption.
